findings.title: Loyalty Points Earning Invisible at Point of Purchase

element.text_content: No points-earning display present anywhere on PDP; Reward Points link exists only in footer

evidence_anchors.context: Footer element (e18, scroll_y=581) contains 'Reward Points' as a plain text link inside the Information column — the only mention of a loyalty program on the entire page. No points-earning indicator, enrollment CTA, or progress display appears anywhere in the above-fold or product hero area.

The footer's Information column lists: Affirm Monthly Payments, About Us, Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Reward Points, CARB Policy. The Reward Points link is the fourth item in a plain unordered list — no visual distinction, no earnings preview, no enrollment prompt.

reference_citations.section: Finding 9: Loyalty Program Enrollment — The Moment of Maximum Receptivity
Finding 1: The Endowed Progress Effect — Artificial Head-Start Increases Completion by 79%
Finding 11: Loyalty Program Display on Confirmation — Points as Positive Reinforcement

findings.observation: The Slingmods store operates a Reward Points program, confirmed by the 'Reward Points' link in the footer and the login prompt noting 'Earn 60 Points when purchasing this item' visible in the product area (e.g., the login/signup bar references this). However, the page does not display how many points a $59.95 purchase earns in a format that reinforces the purchase decision — there is no points-earning indicator adjacent to the price, no enrollment CTA for non-members, and no progress bar or goal-state framing. The only path to the loyalty program from this page is a plain footer link.
findings.recommendation: If a Reward Points program is active, add a points-earning indicator directly below the $59.95 price display — for example, 'Earn 60 points with this purchase ($0.60 toward your next reward)' styled to complement the PayPal Pay Later widget already at that position. For non-logged-in visitors, frame enrollment as a benefit of completing the purchase: 'Sign up free and earn points on this order.' This placement captures the moment of maximum commitment immediately before the Add to Cart action.
findings.why_this_matters: Nunes and Dreze (2006) demonstrated that showing customers they are already making progress toward a reward increases completion rates by 79%. A buyer on the fence between this item and an alternative perceives more total value from a brand that visibly rewards the purchase — suppressing this signal at the highest-intent moment on the page leaves a loyalty-building lever inactive exactly when it costs the most.

findings.title: No Referral CTA Captures 72-Hour Post-Purchase Window

element.text_content: No referral program link or CTA present in footer or elsewhere on page

evidence_anchors.context: Footer element (e18) Extras column lists: Brands, Gift Cards, Dealers, Price Match Guarantee, Sell Your Products on SlingMods. No referral program link, no 'Refer a Friend' CTA, no share mechanism of any kind visible in the footer or anywhere on the PDP.
Footer section shows four columns (Information, Year/Model Charts, Extras, My Account). No referral or friend-sharing element is present in any column or elsewhere in the visible page sections.

reference_citations.section: Finding 6: The 72-Hour Post-Purchase Window Is Peak Referral Opportunity
Finding 1: Double-Sided Incentives Dramatically Outperform Single-Sided

findings.observation: No referral program link, share mechanism, or 'Refer a Friend' CTA appears anywhere on the Slingmods PDP — not in the footer, not adjacent to the product hero, and not in the My Account or Extras navigation columns. Slingmods sells to a niche enthusiast audience (Polaris Slingshot, Can-Am Spyder owners) where word-of-mouth carries above-average weight. The 72-hour window immediately following a purchase is when referral participation peaks — customers are actively checking confirmations and telling others about their purchase.
findings.recommendation: If a referral program does not yet exist, evaluate launching one with double-sided framing ('Give a fellow rider $15 off — you get $15 when they buy'). If a referral program exists but is not surfaced here, add a footer link and embed a lightweight referral prompt in the order confirmation email sequence. For the PDP itself, a subtle 'Tell a fellow rider' social share link near the product title or below the Add to Cart button captures pre-purchase sharing intent from visitors who have already decided to buy.
findings.why_this_matters: Slingmods' customer base is a self-identifying enthusiast community — the same demographic that shares product recommendations in Facebook groups and forums. Talkable platform data shows referral participation jumps 35% when a referral block is added to shipping confirmation emails. Without any referral mechanism, every satisfied customer who would organically share is doing so without a trackable incentive, leaving referral-acquired customer LTV (25% higher profit margin over 3 years per Villanueva et al. 2008) uncaptured.

findings.title: Track My Order Accessible Only via Footer — No Proactive Tracking Prompt

element.text_content: Information Affirm Monthly Payments About Us Privacy Policy Terms & Conditions

evidence_anchors.context: Footer element (e18, scroll_y=581, is_above_fold=false) My Account column contains: My Account, Order History, Wish List, Newsletter, Track My Order. Track My Order is the fifth item in a plain unordered list — present but not promoted. The footer is at scroll_y=1345 per baton rect.y.

Footer My Account column renders Track My Order as an unstyled text link in the same visual weight as Newsletter and Wish List. No tracking CTA or 'You'll receive updates' copy appears near the Add to Cart button in section-1.jpg.

reference_citations.section: Finding 6: Tracking Information Prominence — 96% Check at Least Once
Finding 5: Proactive Tracking Communication Reduces WISMO and Increases Loyalty

findings.observation: A 'Track My Order' link exists in the footer My Account column (below the fold at scroll_y=1345), which is standard footer navigation — it gives existing customers a path to order status. What is missing is any proactive communication about the post-purchase tracking experience at the point of purchase: there is no 'You'll receive shipping updates by email' copy near the Add to Cart button, no mention of when to expect a shipping notification, and no visible indication that order status is accessible via account login or email. The absence is not critical for a PDP, but it represents a missed opportunity to set post-purchase expectations before the buy.
findings.recommendation: If the store sends shipping notification emails automatically, add a one-line reassurance below the Add to Cart button: 'Free shipping on most orders. You'll receive a tracking number by email when your order ships.' This costs zero development effort (a copy-only change in the product template) and directly addresses the post-purchase anxiety that affects 66% of online buyers after clicking buy (Narvar 2025). The Track My Order footer link is correctly placed for returning customers; the pre-purchase copy addresses first-time buyers.
findings.why_this_matters: Narvar's 2025 State of Post-Purchase Report found that 96% of consumers check order tracking at least once, and 63% consider full delivery visibility essential. Setting tracking expectations before purchase reduces the 'where is my order?' support volume that peaks in the 24-48 hours after purchase — particularly important for a niche parts retailer where buyers are often planning a specific ride or event and are more sensitive to delivery timing.

findings.title: My Account Footer Navigation Correctly Structures Post-Purchase Access Points

element.text_content: Information Affirm Monthly Payments About Us Privacy Policy Terms & Conditions

evidence_anchors.context: Footer (e18) My Account column contains: My Account, Order History, Wish List, Newsletter, Track My Order — all five core post-purchase navigation items present.

findings.observation: The footer My Account column includes all standard post-purchase navigation: My Account, Order History, Wish List, Newsletter, and Track My Order. These five links give returning customers direct access to every post-purchase touchpoint without contacting support.
findings.recommendation: Maintain the current My Account footer structure.
findings.why_this_matters: Providing accessible post-purchase navigation reduces support ticket volume from customers who cannot locate order history or tracking links.

notes: This page is a PDP, not an order confirmation page. The post-purchase cluster was routed to the footer section (label: 'Product description, fitment guide and footer', slug: 'product-description-footer') which contains loyalty, referral, and order-tracking navigation signals. Findings are evaluated against the post-purchase touchpoints visible or absent on this PDP, not against a confirmation page template.

The baton's login/signup area (e15, header) references 'Earn 60 Points when purchasing this item' — this suggests a points program is active and IS referenced in the product area, but only for logged-in users. The key gap is visibility for non-logged-in visitors, who see no loyalty signal at all. This nuance is noted but the FAIL finding stands: the points signal is conditional on login state and not visible to the majority of PDP visitors.

The footer payment badge row (e18) correctly displays Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover, Google Pay, Apple Pay, PayPal, Amazon Pay, and Affirm — a complete payment assurance strip that contributes to general trust at the post-purchase stage. Not emitted as a separate PASS finding to avoid redundancy with the trust-credibility specialist.
